A Brief History of Python¶

Python was created by Guido van Rossum in the late 1980s and was first released in 1991. It was designed as a high-level, interpreted language with a focus on code readability and simplicity. The language takes inspiration from ABC, C, and Modula-3, aiming to provide a more approachable and efficient programming experience. Over the years, Python has evolved into one of the most widely used programming languages in data science, web development, automation, and artificial intelligence. Its vast ecosystem of libraries and strong community support make it a preferred choice for statistical analysis and machine learning.

Overview of Python and Jupyter Notebook¶

Python is a versatile and widely used programming language in data analysis, machine learning, and statistical computing. It is popular due to its simplicity, readability, and vast ecosystem of libraries that support numerical computing, data manipulation, and visualization.

Jupyter Notebook is an interactive web-based environment that allows users to write and execute Python code in a document-like format. It supports text, code execution, visualizations, and markdown documentation within a single file.

Installing Python and Jupyter Notebook¶

Python can be installed via:¶

Anaconda Distribution (Recommended): Comes with Python, Jupyter Notebook, and essential libraries pre-installed.

Standalone Python Installation: Using official Python downloads from python.org, followed by manual installation of Jupyter Notebook.

Steps to Install Using Anaconda:¶

Download and install Anaconda.

Open Anaconda Navigator and launch Jupyter Notebook.

Steps to Install Using pip:¶

Install Python from python.org.

Open the terminal or command prompt and run:

Method 1: Install PIP on Windows Using get-pip.py¶

The first method uses cURL to download the installation file and additional configuration steps post-installation. Follow the steps below to install PIP using this methodpt:

Step 1: Download PIP get-pip.py¶

Before installing PIP, download the get-pip.py file. Run the following cURL command in the command prompt:

In [3]:
#curl https://bootstrap.pypa.io/get-pip.py -o get-pip.py

Step 2: Installing PIP on Windows¶

To install PIP, run the following Python command:

In [4]:
#python get-pip.py

Step 3: Verify Installation¶

To test whether the installation was successful, type the following command:

In [5]:
#python -m pip help

Check PIP version installed¶

In [6]:
#pip --version

Now install Jupyter Notebook and Jupyterlab¶

In [7]:
#pip install jupyter
In [8]:
#pip install notebook
In [9]:
#pip install jupyterlab

Check the available jupyter version¶

In [10]:
#jupyter --version

Jupyter Notebook Interface¶

Key components of Jupyter Notebook:

  • Cells: Code and text (Markdown) can be written in separate cells.

  • Kernel: The processing engine that executes code.

  • Output: The results of executed code appear below the respective cell.

Basic Jupyter Notebook Commands

  • Run a cell: Shift + Enter

  • Insert a new cell: Esc + B

  • Delete a cell: Esc + D + D

  • Change cell type (Code/Markdown): Esc + M (for Markdown) or Esc + Y (for Code)

Basic Python Syntax and Data Types¶

Vectors¶

Vectors are one-dimensional data structures that store elements of the same type. They are commonly used in numerical computing and can represent mathematical quantities such as coordinates or time series data. In Python, vectors are typically implemented using lists or specialized numerical libraries such as NumPy.

Lists¶

Lists are ordered, mutable collections that can store elements of different types. They allow for dynamic resizing and provide various methods for element addition, removal, and manipulation. Lists are widely used in data storage, iteration, and flexible data handling.

Matrices¶

Matrices are two-dimensional structures that store data in rows and columns. They are essential in linear algebra, data transformations, and machine learning applications. Matrices facilitate mathematical operations such as addition, multiplication, and inversion. In Python, they are commonly handled using NumPy for efficient computation.

Tuples¶

Tuples are ordered collections similar to lists but are immutable, meaning their elements cannot be modified after creation. They are useful for storing fixed data, ensuring data integrity, and enhancing performance when iteration or access speed is critical.

Dictionaries¶

Dictionaries are data structures that store key-value pairs, allowing for fast data retrieval. They provide a flexible way to organize and access data based on unique keys. Dictionaries are widely used in applications requiring fast lookups, such as databases and data indexing.

Arrays¶

Arrays are homogeneous data structures that store elements of the same type in a contiguous memory location. They offer efficient storage and computation, making them suitable for numerical operations. Unlike lists, arrays require explicit type specification and are often used in scientific computing and large-scale data processing.

This is my first interaction with python (Basic Python Syntax and Codes)¶

Printing Output¶

In [1]:
print("Hello, world")

Hello, world
In [2]:
print(5+10)

15

Python Data types with syntax Examples¶

1. Integer: Represent whole numbers¶

In [3]:
x = 20        # Integer
x
Out[3]:
20

2. Float: Represents decimal numbers¶

In [4]:
y = 3.14      # Float
y
Out[4]:
3.14

3. String: Represents a sequence of characters¶

In [5]:
name = "Mercy" # String
name

greeting = 'Hello'
greeting
Out[5]:
'Hello'

4. Boolean: Represents True or False values¶

In [6]:
is_valid = True # Boolean
is_valid

is_active = False
is_active
Out[6]:
False

5. List: An ordered, mutable collection of items¶

In [7]:
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "orange"]
fruits
print(fruits[2])  # Access 3rd element

orange
In [8]:
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "orange"]
print(fruits[0])  # Access first element
print(fruits[1])  # Access second element
print(fruits[2])  # Access third element

apple
banana
orange

6. Tuple: An ordered, immutable collection of items¶

In [9]:
my_tuple = (1,2,3,"John", True)
my_tuple

coordinates = (10, 20)
print(coordinates[1])  # Access second element

20

7. Set: An unordered mutable collection of unique items¶

In [10]:
my_set = {1,2,3,"John"}
my_set
Out[10]:
{1, 2, 3, 'John'}

8. Dictionary: A collection of key-value pairs¶

In [11]:
my_dict = {
    "name" : "John", 
    "Age": 60,
    "gender": "Male",
    "Occupation": "Doctor"}
my_dict
Out[11]:
{'name': 'John', 'Age': 60, 'gender': 'Male', 'Occupation': 'Doctor'}
In [12]:
person = {"name": "John", "age": 25, "ocupation": "Teacher"}
person
Out[12]:
{'name': 'John', 'age': 25, 'ocupation': 'Teacher'}
In [13]:
print(person["name"])  # Access value by key

John

9. None Type: Represents the absence of a value¶

In [14]:
x = None

10. What are Conditional Statements?¶

Conditional statements execute different code blocks based on whether a condition is True or False.¶

1. if Statement (do something if true)¶

In [15]:
age = 18

if age >= 18:
    print("You are an adult")

You are an adult

2. if-else Statement (do one thing or another)¶

In [16]:
age = 15

if age >= 18:
    print("You are an adult")
else:
    print("You are a minor")

You are a minor

3. if-elif-else Statement (multiple conditions)¶

In [17]:
age = 18
if age >= 18:
    print("Adult")
else:
    print("Minor")

Adult
In [18]:
age = 12
if age >= 18:
    print("Adult")
else:
    print("Minor")

Minor
In [19]:
score = 85

if score >= 90:
    print("Grade: A")
elif score >= 80:
    print("Grade: B")
elif score >= 70:
    print("Grade: C")
else:
    print("Grade: F")

Grade: B

11. What are Loops?¶

Loops execute the same block of code repeatedly until a condition is met. There are two main types: for and while.¶

1. for Loop (repeat a fixed number of times)¶

Basic for loop¶

In [20]:
# For loop
for i in range(5):
    print(i)

0
1
2
3
4

The code above defines a for loop that iterates over a sequence generated by the range(5) function. The range(5) function produces a sequence of numbers starting from 0 up to, but not including, 5, meaning it generates [0, 1, 2, 3, 4]. During each iteration, the variable i takes on the next value in this sequence, and the print(i) statement outputs the current value of i to the console. As a result, the numbers 0, 1, 2, 3, and 4 are printed sequentially. This type of loop is useful when iterating over a known range of values, such as indexing elements in a list or executing a block of code a fixed number of times.

Loop through a list¶

In [21]:
fruits = ['apple', 'banana', 'cherry']

for fruit in fruits:
    print(fruit)

apple
banana
cherry

Loop through a string¶

In [22]:
word = "Python"

for letter in word:
    print(letter)

P
y
t
h
o
n

while Loop (repeat while a condition is True)¶

In [23]:
# While loop (repeat while a condition is True)
count = 0
while count < 3:
    print(count)
    count += 1

0
1
2

The code defines a while loop that continues executing as long as the condition count < 3 is true. Initially, the variable count is set to 0. During each iteration, the current value of count is printed, and then count is incremented by 1 using count += 1. The loop runs until count reaches 3, at which point the condition count < 3 becomes false, and the loop terminates. As a result, the numbers 0, 1, and 2 are printed sequentially. This type of loop is useful when the number of iterations is not predetermined but depends on a condition being met.

Range() Function (create a sequence of numbers)¶

In [24]:
# Count from 1 to 5
for i in range(1, 6):
    print(i)

1
2
3
4
5

Loop Control Statements¶

In [25]:
for i in range(10):
    if i == 5:
        break
    print(i)

0
1
2
3
4

Continue - Skip current iteration¶

In [26]:
for i in range(5):
    if i == 2:
        continue
    print(i)

0
1
3
4

Pass - Do nothing (placeholder)¶

In [27]:
for i in range(3):
    if i == 1:
        pass  # Placeholder for future code
    print(i)

0
1
2

Nested Loops (loop inside a loop)¶

In [28]:
for i in range(3):
    for j in range(2):
        print(f"i={i}, j={j}")

i=0, j=0
i=0, j=1
i=1, j=0
i=1, j=1
i=2, j=0
i=2, j=1

Print a multiplication table¶

In [29]:
for i in range(1, 4):
    for j in range(1, 4):
        print(f"{i} × {j} = {i*j}")

1 × 1 = 1
1 × 2 = 2
1 × 3 = 3
2 × 1 = 2
2 × 2 = 4
2 × 3 = 6
3 × 1 = 3
3 × 2 = 6
3 × 3 = 9

Enumerate() - Get index and value¶

In [30]:
fruits = ['apple', 'banana', 'cherry']

for index, fruit in enumerate(fruits):
    print(f"{index}: {fruit}")

0: apple
1: banana
2: cherry

zip() - Loop through multiple lists¶

In [31]:
names = ['Alice', 'Bob', 'Charlie']
scores = [85, 92, 78]

for name, score in zip(names, scores):
    print(f"{name}: {score}")

Alice: 85
Bob: 92
Charlie: 78

With conditions¶

In [32]:
even_numbers = [i for i in range(10) if i % 2 == 0]
print(even_numbers)

[0, 2, 4, 6, 8]

Data Science Example - Loop through DataFrame¶

In [36]:
import pandas as pd

# Create sample data
data = {
    'name': ['Alice', 'Bob', 'Charlie'],
    'score': [85, 62, 90]
}

df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Loop through rows
for index, row in df.iterrows():
    print(f"{row['name']} scored {row['score']}")

Alice scored 85
Bob scored 62
Charlie scored 90

12. Functions¶

In [24]:
def greet(name):
    return "Hello, " + name

print(greet("Alice"))

Hello, Alice

13. Importing Libraries¶

In [26]:
import math
square_root = math.sqrt(25)

Mathematical Operations¶

1. Basic Arithmetic Operations¶

In [27]:
x = 10
y = 3

addition = x + y       # Addition
subtraction = x - y    # Subtraction
multiplication = x * y # Multiplication
division = x / y       # Division (returns float)
floor_division = x // y # Floor division (integer division)
modulus = x % y        # Modulus (remainder)
exponentiation = x ** y # Exponentiation (power)

print(addition, subtraction, multiplication, division, floor_division, modulus, exponentiation)

13 7 30 3.3333333333333335 3 1 1000

2. Using Built-in Math Functions¶

In [28]:
import math

square_root = math.sqrt(25) # Square root
absolute_value = abs(8)    # Absolute value
power = pow(3, 3)          # 3 raised to the power of 3
logarithm = math.log(10)   # Natural logarithm (ln)
log_base_10 = math.log10(100) # Logarithm base 10
factorial = math.factorial(5) # Factorial of 5

print(square_root, absolute_value, power, logarithm, log_base_10, factorial)

5.0 8 27 2.302585092994046 2.0 120
In [29]:
import math

print(square_root)
print(absolute_value) 
print(power) 
print(logarithm)
print(log_base_10)
print(factorial)

5.0
8
27
2.302585092994046
2.0
120

3. Rounding Functions¶

In [35]:
num = 5.6789
rounded_value = round(num, 2) # Round to 2 decimal places
floor_value = math.floor(num) # Floor (round down)
ceil_value = math.ceil(num)   # Ceiling (round up)

print(rounded_value, floor_value, ceil_value)

5.68 5 6

#!pip install pandas

In [40]:
import pandas as pd                # Importing pandas for data manipulation
import numpy as np                 # Importing NumPy for numerical computations
import seaborn as sns              # Importing seaborn for data visualization
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt    # Import the pyplot module

Working with Vectors in Python¶

In [45]:
import numpy as np

# Creating a vector
vector_a = np.array([1, 2, 3])
vector_a
Out[45]:
array([1, 2, 3])
In [46]:
vector_b = np.array([4, 5, 6])
vector_b
Out[46]:
array([4, 5, 6])

Vector Addition¶

In [47]:
vector_sum = vector_a + vector_b
vector_sum
Out[47]:
array([5, 7, 9])

Vector Subtraction¶

In [48]:
vector_diff = vector_a - vector_b
vector_diff
Out[48]:
array([-3, -3, -3])

Scalar Multiplication¶

In [49]:
scalar_multiplication = 3 * vector_a
scalar_multiplication
Out[49]:
array([3, 6, 9])

Dot Product¶

In [50]:
dot_product = np.dot(vector_a, vector_b)
dot_product
Out[50]:
np.int64(32)

Magnitude of a Vector¶

In [51]:
magnitude_a = np.linalg.norm(vector_a)
magnitude_a
Out[51]:
np.float64(3.7416573867739413)
In [52]:
print("Vector A:", vector_a)
print("Vector B:", vector_b)
print("Vector Sum:", vector_sum)
print("Vector Difference:", vector_diff)
print("Scalar Multiplication:", scalar_multiplication)
print("Dot Product:", dot_product)
print("Magnitude of Vector A:", magnitude_a)

Vector A: [1 2 3]
Vector B: [4 5 6]
Vector Sum: [5 7 9]
Vector Difference: [-3 -3 -3]
Scalar Multiplication: [3 6 9]
Dot Product: 32
Magnitude of Vector A: 3.7416573867739413

Working with Lists in Python¶

Creating a list¶

In [72]:
fruits = ["Apple", "Banana", "Cherry", "Date"]
fruits
Out[72]:
['Apple', 'Banana', 'Cherry', 'Date']

Accessing elements¶

In [73]:
first_fruit = fruits[0]  # Access first element
first_fruit
Out[73]:
'Apple'
In [74]:
last_fruit = fruits[-1]  # Access last element
last_fruit
Out[74]:
'Date'

Modifying elements¶

In [75]:
fruits[1] = "Blueberry"  # Change Banana to Blueberry
fruits
Out[75]:
['Apple', 'Blueberry', 'Cherry', 'Date']

Adding elements¶

In [76]:
fruits.append("Elderberry")  # Append new element to the end
fruits.insert(2, "Coconut")  # Insert at index 2

Removing elements¶

In [77]:
deleted_fruit = fruits.pop(3)  # Remove and return element at index 3
fruits.remove("Date")  # Remove first occurrence of "Apple"
fruits
Out[77]:
['Apple', 'Blueberry', 'Coconut', 'Elderberry']

List slicing¶

In [78]:
subset_fruits = fruits[1:3]  # Get elements from index 1 to 2
subset_fruits
Out[78]:
['Blueberry', 'Coconut']

Iterating over a list¶

In [79]:
for fruit in fruits:
    print(fruit)

Apple
Blueberry
Coconut
Elderberry

Sorting a list¶

In [81]:
fruits.sort()  # Sort alphabetically
fruits
Out[81]:
['Apple', 'Blueberry', 'Coconut', 'Elderberry']

Finding the length of a list¶

In [82]:
num_fruits = len(fruits)
num_fruits
Out[82]:
4
In [83]:
print("Updated Fruits List:", fruits)
print("Deleted Fruit:", deleted_fruit)
print("Subset of Fruits:", subset_fruits)
print("Number of Fruits:", num_fruits)

Updated Fruits List: ['Apple', 'Blueberry', 'Coconut', 'Elderberry']
Deleted Fruit: Cherry
Subset of Fruits: ['Blueberry', 'Coconut']
Number of Fruits: 4

MATRICES¶

Create DataFrame Using Pandas (Most Common)¶

In [158]:
import pandas as pd

# Creating a DataFrame
data = {
    "Name": ["Alice", "Bob", "Charlie", "David",'Dan'],
    "Age": [25, 30, 35, 40, 80],
    "County": ["Embu", "Nyeri", "Busia", "Bomet",'Meru']
}

df = pd.DataFrame(data)
df
Out[158]:
Name Age County
0 Alice 25 Embu
1 Bob 30 Nyeri
2 Charlie 35 Busia
3 David 40 Bomet
4 Dan 80 Meru
In [159]:
import pandas as pd
df = pd.DataFrame(data)
print(df.head())

      Name  Age County
0    Alice   25   Embu
1      Bob   30  Nyeri
2  Charlie   35  Busia
3    David   40  Bomet
4      Dan   80   Meru

Accessing Columns¶

In [160]:
df["County"]
Out[160]:
0     Embu
1    Nyeri
2    Busia
3    Bomet
4     Meru
Name: County, dtype: object

Accessing Rows¶

In [161]:
print("\nAccessing first row:")
print(df.iloc[0])

Accessing first row:
Name      Alice
Age          25
County     Embu
Name: 0, dtype: object

Adding a New Column¶

In [162]:
df["Salary"] = [50000, 60000, 70000, 80000,90000]
df['occupation'] = ['Nurse', 'Teacher', 'Politician', 'Doctor', 'Nurse' ]
df
Out[162]:
Name Age County Salary occupation
0 Alice 25 Embu 50000 Nurse
1 Bob 30 Nyeri 60000 Teacher
2 Charlie 35 Busia 70000 Politician
3 David 40 Bomet 80000 Doctor
4 Dan 80 Meru 90000 Nurse

Filtering Data¶

In [163]:
print("\nFiltering rows where Age > 30:")
df[df["Age"] > 30]

Filtering rows where Age > 30:
Out[163]:
Name Age County Salary occupation
2 Charlie 35 Busia 70000 Politician
3 David 40 Bomet 80000 Doctor
4 Dan 80 Meru 90000 Nurse

Sorting Data¶

In [164]:
df.sort_values(by="Age", ascending=False)
Out[164]:
Name Age County Salary occupation
4 Dan 80 Meru 90000 Nurse
3 David 40 Bomet 80000 Doctor
2 Charlie 35 Busia 70000 Politician
1 Bob 30 Nyeri 60000 Teacher
0 Alice 25 Embu 50000 Nurse

Grouping Data¶

In [165]:
df.groupby("County")["Age"].mean()
Out[165]:
County
Bomet    40.0
Busia    35.0
Embu     25.0
Meru     80.0
Nyeri    30.0
Name: Age, dtype: float64

Handling Missing Data¶

In [166]:
df.loc[2, "Salary"] = None  # Introduce a missing value (numerical)
df
Out[166]:
Name Age County Salary occupation
0 Alice 25 Embu 50000.0 Nurse
1 Bob 30 Nyeri 60000.0 Teacher
2 Charlie 35 Busia NaN Politician
3 David 40 Bomet 80000.0 Doctor
4 Dan 80 Meru 90000.0 Nurse
In [167]:
print("\nDataFrame with missing value:")
df

DataFrame with missing value:
Out[167]:
Name Age County Salary occupation
0 Alice 25 Embu 50000.0 Nurse
1 Bob 30 Nyeri 60000.0 Teacher
2 Charlie 35 Busia NaN Politician
3 David 40 Bomet 80000.0 Doctor
4 Dan 80 Meru 90000.0 Nurse
In [168]:
print("\nFilling missing values with mean salary:")
df["Salary"].fillna(df["Salary"].mean(), inplace=True)
df

Filling missing values with mean salary:
Out[168]:
Name Age County Salary occupation
0 Alice 25 Embu 50000.0 Nurse
1 Bob 30 Nyeri 60000.0 Teacher
2 Charlie 35 Busia 70000.0 Politician
3 David 40 Bomet 80000.0 Doctor
4 Dan 80 Meru 90000.0 Nurse

Suppress Warnings¶

In [169]:
import warnings

# Suppress all warnings
warnings.simplefilter("ignore")

print("\nFilling missing values with mean salary:")
df["Salary"].fillna(df["Salary"].mean(), inplace=True)
df

Filling missing values with mean salary:
Out[169]:
Name Age County Salary occupation
0 Alice 25 Embu 50000.0 Nurse
1 Bob 30 Nyeri 60000.0 Teacher
2 Charlie 35 Busia 70000.0 Politician
3 David 40 Bomet 80000.0 Doctor
4 Dan 80 Meru 90000.0 Nurse
In [170]:
df.loc[2, "occupation"] = None  # Introduce a missing value (Categorical)
df
Out[170]:
Name Age County Salary occupation
0 Alice 25 Embu 50000.0 Nurse
1 Bob 30 Nyeri 60000.0 Teacher
2 Charlie 35 Busia 70000.0 None
3 David 40 Bomet 80000.0 Doctor
4 Dan 80 Meru 90000.0 Nurse
In [172]:
import warnings

# Suppress all warnings
warnings.simplefilter("ignore")

df["occupation"].fillna(df["occupation"].mode()[0], inplace=True) # Replace the mising value
df
Out[172]:
Name Age County Salary occupation
0 Alice 25 Embu 50000.0 Nurse
1 Bob 30 Nyeri 60000.0 Teacher
2 Charlie 35 Busia 70000.0 Nurse
3 David 40 Bomet 80000.0 Doctor
4 Dan 80 Meru 90000.0 Nurse

# Exporting Data to CSV
df.to_csv("output.csv", index=False)
